summary: Stancil argues the “data as a product” idea is incomplete without a clear definition of what counts as production. Because data work often blurs one-off analysis and durable assets, teams end up maintaining a sprawling, accidental production surface. He proposes explicitly declaring production assets and letting everything else be temporary, with real support and deprecation for the chosen few.
money_quotes: If data is a product, we should have a better definition of production.
Production is a fuzzy concept, with a wide and blurry boundary between it and development.
When I send a report like this to someone, there’s an implicit contract attached to it: It works now, for the thing I said it would work for. It is, at that moment, in production.
We should keep production narrow and exclusive, and be content going days and weeks at a time without ever shipping anything to it.
To paraphrase a favorite data cliché, that which is defined is maintained.
key_insight: Data teams need an explicit, narrow definition of production so they can support what matters and let the rest expire instead of growing an unmanageable, accidental production surface.
